Title:
I’ve just gone through sleep data from 50+ racers and it’s honestly worrying.

Over 80% can’t wake up without an alarm.
Around 70% need caffeine before midday just to feel normal.
Most of them could fall back asleep at 10am without trying.

Now imagine taking that brain onto track.

Here’s what that actually means when you’re riding:

Your reaction time is 30–50 milliseconds slower

You’re missing visual cues that should feel obvious

Your brain can’t keep up when something suddenly changes

That “off” feeling you sometimes get?

The corner where your timing was weird

The moment you froze instead of rolling on

The near-miss you can’t quite explain

That usually isn’t bad luck.
That’s bad sleep.

When you’re sleep deprived, the decision-making part of your brain is the first thing to switch off.
So when the car slides, or someone runs you tight, your brain is a step behind and you feel it as hesitation, tension or panic.

I’ve seen this over and over working with racers.

The ones who keep progressing, session after session, aren’t just “naturally confident”.
They take recovery as seriously as your setup.

Sleep is the cleanest performance mod you’ll ever get.
It’s legal. It’s free.
And almost every racer is leaving performance on the table by ignoring it.

The good bit:

You can start turning this around in the next 7–14 days with the right protocol.
I’ve already used it with drivers who went from wired and foggy to calm and switched on in under two weeks.

The bad bit:

Most drivers won’t change anything until they’ve already scared themselves or ended up in the gravel.

Want the fix? DM Blueprint below and I’ll send it over.

How racing drivers can supercharge their performance 🏎

Most drivers focus on:
✅ The best car
✅ The best setup  
✅ The best equipment

But what happens when everyone on the grid has invested at the same level?

What separates you from the rest?

The mental approach.

This is the void in motorsport that's keeping talented drivers stuck mid-pack.

I'm breaking down the exact 3-step mental performance system that's taken drivers from mid-pack to podiums.

Title:
Big Mistake #4: Trying too hard 🏎️💭

"I just need to push harder."
"I need to try more."
"I need to give 110%."

Here's the problem:

When you try harder, you create MORE tension.

More tension = slower lap times.

The harder you try, the slower you go.

Your conscious brain (110 bits/second) is trying to override your subconscious brain (4 BILLION bits/second).

You can't out-think 4 billion bits of processing power.

The answer? Stop trying so hard.

Let your subconscious do what it's designed to do.

Want to learn how to access your subconscious speed?

Title:
Big Mistake #5: The Frustration Loop 🏎️💭

You make a mistake.

You get frustrated.

The frustration makes you tense.

The tension makes you make MORE mistakes.

Now you're in a loop:

Mistake → Frustration → Tension → More mistakes

One bad corner ruins your entire race.

The fix?

Learn to break the frustration loop BEFORE it costs you the race.

Want to learn how to stay composed when things go wrong?

Title:
Big Mistake #7: Superstitions 🏎️🍀
Lucky underwear.
Left glove on first.
Fist bump before the race.
The paddock is FULL of superstitions.

There's no science behind it.

But you keep wearing them because "what if I don't?"
It's crazy. It's proven NOT to work.

Title:
🧠 THE 7 SIGNS YOU'RE IN FLOW STATE 🧠

Ever had a session where everything just... clicks?

That's flow state. And it's not luck—it's science.

When you're in flow:

✅ Everything happens automatically—no thinking
✅ Your inner critic goes silent (finally!)
✅ Time warps—40 minutes feels like 5
✅ You KNOW when to make the move—pure intuition
✅ You're on the limit with ease and joy
✅ Your memory gets fuzzy (can't remember epic overtakes!)
✅ The experience itself is the reward

The problem? Most drivers only hit flow by accident.

The solution? There's a proven process to access it on demand.

I've worked with drivers across multiple race series to unlock their flow state triggers.

The car is moving around, you're having slides, you're coping—but it all feels manageable.

People ask: "How did you overtake those 3 cars in turn one?"

You answer: "I don't know." Because your memory was distorted in that flow activity.

The byproduct? Podiums. Pole positions. Race wins.

But the experience itself is so rewarding that you're not even focused on the outcome.

When you're in flow, you're pretty hard to beat.

👉 DM "BLUEPRINT" for instant access to our free driver training that shows you how to enter flow state on command.

#FlowState #RacingDrivers #Motorsport #TrackDay #PeakPerformance #MentalStrength #RaceReady #DriverCoaching #F4 #RacingMindset

Title:
What is Flow State? 🏎️🧠

25 years ago, flow was seen as mystical.

Something from meditation and martial arts.

High discipline. High dedication.

Then Red Bull changed everything.

With EEG technology and action sports athletes, we could finally SEE what was happening in the brain during peak performance.

Now we know:

✅ Skiing = high flow environment
✅ Surfing = high flow environment
✅ Snowboarding = high flow environment
✅ Playing music = flow environment
✅ Playing with your dog = flow environment

And YES - car racing is a HIGH flow environment.

Flow isn't mystical anymore.

It's science. It's measurable. It's trainable.

Want to learn how to access flow on command?

Title:
🥴 Meet the Drunken Monkey - the voice that sabotages 90% of your races

You know that voice in your head that says:
• "Brake earlier or you'll bin it"
• "That driver is faster than you'll ever be"
• "You should've made that pass 3 corners ago"

That's your Drunken Monkey.

It jumps to conclusions. Predicts the future (and gets it wrong). Creates chaos both on and off track. Makes you overthink every corner.

And it's costing you lap time.

The fastest drivers don't have a quieter monkey - they've just learned to stop letting it drive the car.

Want to know how? Comment "Blueprint" and I'll send you our free training that shows you exactly how your monkey is holding you back.

Title:
🧠 WHY FLOW STATE DISAPPEARS (AND HOW TO GET IT BACK) 🧠

The problem with flow? It's elusive.

Some drivers only get flow ONCE in a season.

It appears out of nowhere. Boosts your performance 10X. You're faster than ever. Making split-second decisions—all the right ones. Car's on rails. Lap record pace. Feels fantastic.

You leave that weekend loving the experience, looking forward to that level at the next round.

But then it disappears just as fast as it arrived.

Next round? Struggling to find your pace. Flow is nowhere to be found.

THE NEUROSCIENCE REASON: FLOW BLOCKERS

❌ Distractions (internal/external)
❌ Challenge too high (anxiety kicks you out)
❌ Avoiding mistakes (driving at 70-80% = no flow)
❌ Looking for flow (trying to force it pushes it away)

THE SOLUTION: Track more than just the car.

This is the F4 sim at Arden Motorsport. The tech on the left reads brainwaves—showing exactly how close or far the driver is from flow.

FIRST RUN:

High brain activity (solid band)
Over-trying, frustrated
Mistake at 8min → tries to recover → bigger mistake
Spikes to 300 microvolts, then 500 microvolts
"That's as fast as I can go. I don't know how anyone goes faster."
SECOND RUN (after Camino Process):

21 minutes averaging 50 microvolts (not 100—FIFTY)
In flow the ENTIRE session
Feels easy
Improved lap time by 3/10ths
"There's more opportunity. I can improve."
Same driver. Same track. Different mental state.

👉 DM "BLUEPRINT" for instant access to our free driver training that shows you how to eliminate flow blockers and access flow on command.

#FlowState #RacingDrivers #Motorsport #F4 #Neuroscience #BrainwaveData #ArdenMotorsport #PeakPerformance #CaminoCoaching #RacingMindset

In 2006, Stanford researchers discovered something that explains why some drivers improve every season while others plateau for years.
When you make a mistake on track, your brain generates two signals.
The first detects the error. Everyone's brain does this.
The second determines how much attention you allocate to learning from that error.
Here's the finding that changes everything:
Growth mindset drivers show significantly larger learning signals.
Fixed mindset drivers dismiss mistakes quickly and move on.
Same mistake.
Same corner.
Same car.
Completely different brain responses.
Those responses directly predict who breaks through and who stays stuck.
The research also proves: this neural pattern is trainable.
I've put together a free training that breaks down exactly what's happening in your brain under pressure—and gives you one protocol to shift the pattern in your very next session.

Jason Plato didn't leave the house for 6 months.
Took down every mirror. Hated the person looking back at him.
This is a two-time British Touring Car Champion. 97 race wins. One of the biggest names the BTCC has ever seen.
And within six weeks of his final race weekend, he lost his identity completely.
His TV work stopped. Investments went wrong. Marriage broke down. Four pillars collapsed at once.
Why am I telling you this?
Because Plato said something in a recent ITV interview that should make every club racer uncomfortable.
He said: "Ever since karting, emotion is the enemy. I became very good at blocking emotion out and thought I had everything under control."
Sound familiar?
That ability to compartmentalise — to block everything out and just drive — it works brilliantly inside the car.
But it creates a dangerous dependency.
Your entire identity becomes "I am a racing driver." Your whole mental architecture is built on one pillar. And when that pillar wobbles — a bad weekend, a budget crisis, a missed championship — the whole structure collapses.
I've seen it across 10 seasons in paddocks from F4 to F1. The drivers who perform most consistently aren't the ones who block everything out. They're the ones who've built a mental operating system with multiple foundations.
They can have a disaster in qualifying and start the race with complete confidence. They can get punted wide on Lap 1 and be mentally reset by Turn 3.
Not because they don't feel it. Because they've engineered how they process it.
Plato's back in the BTCC paddock for 2026 as a team owner. He found his way through the darkness. But he'll tell you himself — he wishes he'd built that foundation decades earlier.
Your identity shouldn't rest entirely on what happens between green light and chequered flag.
Because when the results aren't there, you need something solid underneath you.
·· Oh, by the way — after every race weekend, I send a free Race Weekend Review to drivers who want to know exactly where they left performance on the table. 3-minute assessment that scores your preparation, focus, and mental approach across every session. Your qualifying session starts in 45 minutes. You're sat in the paddock scrolling Instagram.
Seems harmless. Just killing time.
Research published this week reviewed what smartphone scrolling does to athletic performance. Just one hour of scrolling impairs your decision-making, reaction time, and capacity for at least 30 minutes. Some studies suggest over two hours.
Cortisol rises. Heart rate variability drops. Self-regulation gets depleted before you've turned a wheel.
Now translate that to your race weekend.
You're checking competitor posts. Timing feeds. Onboards. WhatsApp. Weather app. Feels productive. None of it is.
By the time you climb in for FP1, your reaction speed at turn-in points is already compromised. Your ability to read what the car is telling you through the wheel is dulled.
You're spending £2,500 to £5,000 per weekend. You get maybe 20 minutes in FP1 to find a baseline. If your brain needs 30 minutes to recover from scrolling, you've burned your first session before the car left pit lane.
Phone in the bag 90 minutes before any session. Not on silent. In the bag.
The 27% of drivers who have a pre-session routine are gaining an advantage before the engine fires. The other 73% are handing it to them while staring at a screen.

F1 just admitted they don't know if their own drivers can handle the mental workload.
The 2026 regulations introduce active aero that drivers must manually manage across multiple zones every single lap. Not like DRS where you press a button once on the straight. Multiple activations. Every lap. Plus managing 350kW of electrical power deployment and harvesting.

The FIA's own head of single-seaters, Nikolas Tombazis, said what one driver finds manageable, another might not. Different drivers have different mental bandwidths.
Read that again. Formula 1 is openly acknowledging that cognitive overload is a real performance limiter.

Now think about your last 60-minute stint in club racing or GT.
You're managing tyre degradation. Fuel load changes altering the balance. Traffic. Pit window timing. Flag signals. Weather shifts. A car that felt planted on lap 3 is understeering by lap 15 and you're adjusting your driving on the fly.

Nobody handed you a briefing document four weeks in advance telling you where the tricky bits would be. Nobody's monitoring your cognitive load from a pit wall.
You're processing all of it alone in the cockpit. In real time. While carrying corner speed through Paddock Hill Bend.

The drivers who handle this well aren't the ones with bigger brains. They're the ones who've automated the basics so their conscious mind has bandwidth left for the decisions that matter.

When your braking points, racing line, and throttle application run on autopilot, you free up mental capacity for the stuff that actually changes during a stint. That's where races are won. Not in raw pace. In cognitive headroom.

If F1 is worried about it, maybe it's worth asking how well you're managing yours.

You're approaching the 100-metre board at 140mph.
You know you can brake later. Your data says so. You've done it in practice.
But in qualifying, with the championship gap closing, your brain starts thinking.
"Smooth initial pressure, squeeze harder, trail off into the apex, don't lock the front left like last time..."

You've turned a skill your body already owns into a conscious checklist. The lock-up happens. The lap is gone.

Researchers at UC Santa Barbara tested what actually causes choking under pressure.
They split participants into two groups. One trained with pre-planned conscious cues. The driver who mentally rehearses every pedal input. The other trained incidentally. They learned the skill by feel.

Then they cranked up the pressure.
The conscious group choked. Movement variability increased. In racing terms, inconsistent pedal application, unpredictable inputs, lock-ups that weren't there in free practice.
The incidental group? Immune.

Both groups reached the same skill level. The difference was how much conscious knowledge they carried about the skill itself.
Think about your best-ever lap. I guarantee you weren't thinking about brake pressure or steering angle. You were just driving. Your conscious brain had the good sense to stay out of the way.

Now think about your worst qualifying session. Where you were trying to be fast instead of letting fast happen.

That's the explicit monitoring trap. Your brain, under pressure, grabs the steering wheel from your subconscious. And your subconscious is a far better driver than your thinking mind will ever be.

Your body knows how to brake at the 100-metre board. The question is whether your brain will let it.

Your phone is costing you lap time. And you don't even know it.
New research just confirmed what I've been seeing in paddocks for years. One to two hours of smartphone scrolling before physical activity produces measurable performance declines that last 30 minutes or more.
Not "feeling a bit sluggish." Actual, measurable drops.
Reduced decision-making speed. Suppressed self-regulation. Elevated cortisol. Impaired reaction capacity. And here's the one that should worry you, the same physical effort felt harder. Your brain was telling you that you were working at maximum when you had more to give.
Now think about what most drivers do between sessions.
Sat in the awning. Scrolling Instagram. Checking WhatsApp. Watching someone else's onboard on TikTok. Refreshing timing screens obsessively.
Then they strap in for qualifying and wonder why the braking markers feel late. Why they're locking fronts into Turn 1. Why the car "just doesn't feel right" compared to FP2.
It's not the car. It's not the tyres. It's not setup.
It's the 45 minutes of scrolling you did between sessions. Your prefrontal cortex, the bit responsible for processing braking references, reading grip levels, and making split-second decisions at 120mph, was already depleted before you left the pit lane.
The fix is simple. Put the phone in the car between sessions. Not on silent. Not face down. In the car. Out of reach.
Walk the paddock. Sit with your eyes closed for five minutes. Review your sector notes on paper. Talk to your engineer about the next session plan.
Do anything except scroll.
Your competitors won't do this. That's your advantage.

F1 just accidentally proved what's been costing you lap time for years.
During the 2026 Barcelona shakedown, every single driver climbed out of the car and said the same thing. The new regulations are demanding more from their brains than ever before.
Multiple wing activation zones per lap. Manual control of front and rear aero. 350kW of electrical power to deploy and harvest. A new overtake mode to manage. All at 180mph.
The FIA's head of single-seaters admitted something fascinating. He said what one driver finds totally manageable, another driver finds overwhelming.
Same car. Same regulations. Same track. Different mental capacity.
Sound familiar?
Here's the thing. Those F1 drivers have the best engineers, the best simulators, and months of preparation to handle extra cognitive load.
You don't.
And yet most club racers are already trying to process way more than their conscious mind can handle. Worrying about the car behind. Checking gaps. Overthinking braking markers. Running a commentary in your helmet about what's going wrong.
Your conscious mind processes 110 bits of information per second. Your subconscious handles 4 billion.
Every time you add another conscious task, whether that's worrying about tyre deg, obsessing over the timing screen, or second-guessing your line, you're asking your 110-bit processor to do a 4-billion-bit job.
F1 is deliberately increasing driver workload because they want the best drivers to stand out.
The question is, are you deliberately reducing yours?
Because the fastest drivers I work with aren't processing more. They're processing less. They've automated the noise so their subconscious can do what it does best.
When was the last time you were actually quiet behind the wheel?

Carnegie Mellon University just figured out why you get slower when you try harder.
They recorded the actual neurons responsible for motor control while people performed precise tasks under pressure. When the stakes were normal, performance was fine. When the stakes shot up, performance collapsed.
Not gradually. Collapsed.
Here's the bit that should worry you.
The researchers found that increasing motivation improves neural signal quality up to a point. Beyond that point, neural activity spreads away from the optimal execution zones in your brain. Your signals become noisy. Your precision disappears.
They called it "information collapse" and they could see it happening in windows of a few hundred milliseconds.
Sound familiar?
That championship decider where you've spent £4,000 on the weekend and you need the points. The qualifying lap where P3 means front row and P4 means second row in traffic.
Your brain literally changes how it fires when the stakes get too high.
That's why you brake earlier than you did in FP2. That's why the racing line that felt automatic on Friday suddenly needs conscious thought on Sunday. That's why the lap time you found in testing vanishes the moment it matters.
You're not bottling it. Your neurons are collapsing under the weight of what you've told yourself this lap means.
The fix isn't motivation. It's learning to compete without raising the internal stakes so high that your brain's motor system can't do its job.
The drivers I work with across F4, GB3 and GT racing learn to decouple the result from the process. Not because it sounds nice on a podcast. Because the neuroscience says your brain performs best when it's not monitoring itself.

Your best ever qualifying lap probably happened when your brain was doing less, not more.
That sounds wrong. But the neuroscience backs it up completely.
There's a process called transient hypofrontality. Bit of a mouthful. What it means is simple. During peak performance, your prefrontal cortex, the part of your brain responsible for self-awareness, conscious decision-making and internal dialogue, temporarily goes quiet.
It doesn't switch off. It steps back.
Your brain stops checking its own work. It stops asking "am I braking late enough?" and "is this the right line?" and "what's the gap to P3?" It lets the skills you've spent years building run without interference.
That's the zone. That's flow.
Every driver I've worked with across F4, GB3 and GT racing describes it the same way. "I wasn't thinking. I was just driving." They come in after the session and can barely remember specific corners. But the data trace is the cleanest they've ever produced.
Here's the strange part.
You can't think your way into that state. The harder you concentrate, the more active your prefrontal cortex becomes. The more it monitors. The more it interferes.
That's why your best laps come when you feel like you're driving at 95%, not 110%. Your brain has stopped second-guessing every input and let muscle memory take the racing line through the apex.
The question isn't whether you've experienced it. Most drivers have, at least once.
The question is whether you know what triggered it and whether you can access it again on demand.
When was the last time you came in from a session and thought "I have no idea how I just did that"?

Your brain can track one object at 7 updates per second.
Add a second thing to focus on? It drops to 4.
Add a third? Under 3.
That's a 63% collapse in processing speed. Published in the Journal of Vision, not a blog post.
Now think about what you're asking your brain to do mid-stint.
You're tracking the braking marker ahead. You're glancing at the mirror because the car behind closed half a second in the last sector. And you're processing the gap to the car in front through the timing screen on the pit wall.
Three targets. Your brain just went from sharp to blurred.
That mirror sweep you thought was just "being aware"? It cost you temporal resolution on the thing that actually matters. Your braking point. Your apex. Your reference speed.
Your brain would literally perform better if you ignored the car behind and committed 100% to hitting your marks.
This is the bit most drivers don't want to hear.
Defending isn't free. Every glance in the mirror reduces the precision of what you see ahead. Not by a little. By more than half.
The fastest drivers I've worked with across 1,644 PBs have one thing in common. They don't split attention. They commit to the corner they're in and trust the mirrors for specific moments, not constant surveillance.
You're not losing tenths because you lack car control. You're losing tenths because you're trying to watch three things at once and your brain physically cannot keep up.
